research_series.series_name: Post-Viral States, Long COVID, Immune Activation, Dysautonomia, Fatigue, Brain Fog, and Cognition

patterns.pattern_name: Post-viral syndromes are associated with persistent multisystem fatigue, brain fog, dyspnea, sleep disturbance, pain, and PEM

mediator.name: multisystem_post_infectious_dysregulation
mediator.pathway: infection-triggered multisystem perturbation involving immune, autonomic, vascular, metabolic, sleep, and neurocognitive domains
mediator.mechanism: persistent symptoms may reflect combined immune activation, dysautonomia, endothelial dysfunction, altered neurocognitive processing, sleep disruption, and impaired exertional recovery

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: A post-viral or long-COVID state increases the likelihood of persistent multisystem symptoms, especially fatigue, brain fog, sleep disturbance, dyspnea, pain, and exercise intolerance, but mechanisms are heterogeneous.

hypotheses.reason: Large reviews and cohorts consistently report persistent fatigue, brain fog/cognitive symptoms, sleep disturbance, dyspnea, pain, and PEM months after infection.

evidence.summary: Persistent fatigue, dyspnea, brain fog, sleep disturbance, pain/myalgia, mood symptoms, and PEM are common after viral infections including SARS-CoV-2. Symptoms cluster into subphenotypes and affect both hospitalized and non-hospitalized patients.

key_sources.title: SARS-CoV-2 post-acute sequelae in previously hospitalised patients: systematic literature review and meta-analysis
key_sources.relevant_finding: Persistent fatigue and multisystem symptoms are common after acute infection.
key_sources.limitations: heterogeneity across cohorts and symptom definitions

key_sources.title: Mid and long-term neurological and neuropsychiatric manifestations of post-COVID-19 syndrome: A meta-analysis
key_sources.relevant_finding: Neurological and neuropsychiatric symptoms persist after COVID-19.

limitations: Symptom reports may not align with objective biomarkers
Causality from specific immune signatures to symptoms remains incompletely proven

patterns.pattern_name: Post-infectious dysautonomia and POTS are common in long COVID and ME/CFS-like illness

mediator.name: autonomic_dysfunction
mediator.pathway: post-infectious autonomic regulation failure affecting orthostatic tolerance, vagal activity, sympathetic activity, and small fiber function
mediator.mechanism: reduced HRV, impaired parasympathetic activity, exaggerated orthostatic responses, sympathetic overactivation, and small fiber neuropathy may contribute to fatigue, dizziness, brain fog, and exercise intolerance

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: After viral infection, dysautonomia/POTS-like patterns increase the likelihood of fatigue, dizziness, brain fog, and exercise intolerance, especially when symptoms worsen upright or after exertion.

hypotheses.reason: Autonomic testing studies show high rates of dysautonomia/POTS and HRV abnormalities in long COVID and ME/CFS compared with controls.

evidence.summary: Multiple autonomic testing studies report dysautonomia/POTS, reduced HRV, impaired vagal activity, exaggerated orthostatic responses, sympathetic overactivation, and small fiber neuropathy in long COVID/ME-CFS-like states.

key_sources.title: Dysautonomia and small fiber neuropathy in post-COVID condition and Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
key_sources.relevant_finding: Dysautonomia and small fiber neuropathy are common in post-COVID condition and ME/CFS.

limitations: Autonomic findings differ by test protocol and phenotype
Causality and directionality are not always established
POTS-like symptoms overlap with dehydration, medication, anxiety, and deconditioning

patterns.pattern_name: Persistent immune dysregulation and interferon/T-cell alterations are associated with long COVID symptoms

mediator.name: immune_dysregulation
mediator.pathway: persistent inflammatory/interferon and adaptive immune activation after infection
mediator.mechanism: elevated IFN signatures, altered T-cell subsets, persistent CD8 activation/proliferation, autoantibodies, and endothelial dysfunction may contribute to fatigue and neurocognitive symptoms in subgroups

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Persistent immune dysregulation after infection can increase the likelihood of fatigue and brain fog, but standard inflammatory markers may be normal and mechanisms differ by subgroup.

hypotheses.reason: Longitudinal immune profiling reports persistent interferon/T-cell changes, but symptom mechanisms are heterogeneous and standard CRP/IL-6 can be normal.

evidence.summary: Long COVID and ME/CFS show immune dysregulation including interferon signaling, altered T-cell subsets, persistent T-cell activation/proliferation, and in some cases neural autoantibodies. However, standard markers like CRP/IL-6 may be normal despite severe symptoms.

key_sources.title: Distinguishing features of long COVID identified through immune profiling
key_sources.relevant_finding: Immune profiling identifies distinguishing features of long COVID.

key_sources.title: Long COVID manifests with T cell dysregulation, inflammation and an uncoordinated adaptive immune response to SARS-CoV-2
key_sources.relevant_finding: Long COVID associated with T-cell dysregulation and immune activation.
key_sources.limitations: causal chain to symptoms not fully established

limitations: Standard inflammatory markers can be normal
Autoantibodies may define only a subgroup
Full causal chains from immune signature to fatigue/brain fog remain incomplete
alternative_explanations: mood symptoms as mediator or consequence

patterns.pattern_name: Objective cognitive deficits are present in post-viral states but subjective brain fog does not always correlate with testing or imaging

mediator.name: neurocognitive_and_neuroimaging_marker_variability
mediator.pathway: post-viral neurocognitive dysfunction may involve attention, memory, executive deficits and variable imaging/electrophysiological findings
mediator.mechanism: fatigue, depression, autonomic dysfunction, sleep disruption, neurovascular changes, and neural network dysfunction may contribute to subjective and objective impairment

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Post-viral brain fog may include objective attention, memory, and executive deficits, but subjective complaints, cognitive testing, and imaging are not interchangeable and may dissociate.

hypotheses.reason: Cognitive testing shows deficits in some cohorts, while imaging findings and subjective-objective correlations are inconsistent.

evidence.summary: Objective cognitive deficits in attention, memory, and executive function are documented in post-viral cohorts, but subjective brain fog does not always correlate with objective cognitive performance or neuroimaging.

key_sources.title: Unraveling brain fog in post-COVID syndrome
key_sources.relevant_finding: Subjective cognitive complaints relate variably to cognitive function, fatigue, and neuropsychiatric symptoms.

limitations: Brain fog is not a single cognitive construct
Testing tools may miss functional cognitive fatigue

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Normal standard inflammatory markers do not exclude post-viral fatigue or brain fog.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Reports show CRP/IL-6 or other standard inflammatory measures may be normal despite severe symptoms.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Subjective brain fog is not equivalent to objective cognitive impairment.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Subjective complaints, cognitive testing, fatigue, mood symptoms, and imaging findings can dissociate.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: A single mechanism cannot explain all long COVID or ME/CFS-like cases.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Post-viral syndromes are heterogeneous across immune, autonomic, endothelial, respiratory, metabolic, and neurocognitive domains.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: Persistent physiological/neurobehavioral state after acute viral infection.
Post-acute sequelae after SARS-CoV-2 infection with persistent multisystem symptoms.
Chronic fatigue syndrome-like illness characterized by fatigue, PEM, cognitive symptoms, and multisystem dysfunction.
Delayed symptom exacerbation after physical or cognitive exertion.
Autonomic dysfunction following infection, including POTS-like patterns and HRV abnormalities.
Persistent activation or dysregulation of immune pathways after infection.
Normal routine inflammatory markers despite persistent symptoms.

research_gaps: Causal links between specific immune signatures and fatigue/brain fog remain unclear.
Pediatric cohorts, autonomic testing, immune biomarkers, and neuroimaging are unevenly represented.
Objective biomarkers predicting prognosis or treatment response are not yet reliable.
Need longitudinal full-chain studies measuring infection history, immune/autonomic/endothelial mediators, and neurobehavioral outcomes.

most_supported_patterns: Persistent fatigue and cognitive symptoms are common after viral infection.
Autonomic dysfunction/POTS-like patterns are prevalent in long COVID and ME/CFS-like illness.
Immune dysregulation can persist months after infection.
highest_value_patterns_for_inference: standard inflammatory markers normal does not exclude post-viral fatigue
patterns_requiring_caution: immune_activation -> fatigue as a direct causal chain
autoantibodies -> symptom severity as a general mechanism
subjective brain fog -> objective cognitive impairment
recommended_next_queries: allostatic load and recovery failure as cross-domain mechanism
post-exertional malaise and delayed symptom amplification
wearable markers of post-viral autonomic dysfunction
summary.overall_assessment: Post-viral syndromes are a high-value v1 graph domain because they integrate immune, autonomic, vascular, respiratory, metabolic, sleep, and cognitive branches. The strongest practical inference is not a single biomarker, but a pattern: infection history plus persistent fatigue/brain fog plus orthostatic intolerance/PEM/autonomic or immune abnormalities.

works_cited.title: Dysautonomia and small fiber neuropathy in post-COVID condition and Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
Distinguishing features of long COVID identified through immune profiling
Unraveling brain fog in post-COVID syndrome
